Helix.name: Helix
Helix.function: The curved outer rim of the visible ear (the auricle). Its folds aren't just decoration — they subtly shape and funnel incoming sound toward the ear canal, and the tiny direction-dependent changes they make to a sound help your brain tell whether it came from above, below or behind.

Antihelix.name: Antihelix
Antihelix.function: The inner Y-shaped ridge that runs parallel to the helix. Together with the helix it forms the springy elastic-cartilage frame that gives the ear its shape and gently channels sound into the bowl of the concha.

Concha of auricle.name: Concha
Concha of auricle.function: The deep central bowl of the auricle that sits right at the entrance to the ear canal. It acts like a small acoustic dish, collecting sound and resonating with mid-range frequencies — the band most important for understanding speech — before passing it inward.

Lobule of auricle.name: Earlobe (Lobule)
Lobule of auricle.function: The soft fleshy lower part of the ear. Unlike the rest of the auricle it contains no cartilage, just fatty tissue and a rich blood supply, which is why it is warm, flexible and the usual place for a piercing. It plays little role in hearing itself.

Tympanic membrane.name: Eardrum (Tympanic membrane)
Tympanic membrane.function: A thin, taut cone of skin and fibrous tissue at the end of the ear canal. Sound waves make it vibrate like a drumskin, converting airborne sound into mechanical movement and passing it to the tiny bones behind it. It is the boundary between the outer and middle ear.

Malleus.name: Malleus (Hammer)
Malleus.function: The first and largest of the three middle-ear bones (ossicles). Its handle is attached to the eardrum, so when the drum vibrates the malleus moves with it and hands the vibration on to the incus. Its name is Latin for 'hammer'.

Incus.name: Incus (Anvil)
Incus.function: The middle ossicle, shaped like an anvil. It forms the bridge between the malleus and the stapes, relaying and helping to lever the vibration along the chain so almost none of the sound energy is lost crossing the air-filled middle ear.

Stapes.name: Stapes (Stirrup)
Stapes.function: The third ossicle and the smallest bone in the human body. Its stirrup-shaped footplate pushes on the oval window of the inner ear, transferring the focused vibration into the cochlear fluid. The ossicle chain acts as a lever that amplifies the pressure about 20-fold.

Auditory tube.name: Eustachian Tube
Auditory tube.function: A narrow passage connecting the middle ear to the back of the throat. Normally closed, it pops open when you swallow or yawn to equalise air pressure on both sides of the eardrum — the 'ear popping' you feel on a plane or going up a hill.

Cochlea.name: Cochlea
Cochlea.function: The snail-shaped, fluid-filled hearing organ. Vibrations ripple through its fluid and bend tiny hair cells lining its spiral, which fire nerve signals to the brain. It is laid out by pitch: the base detects high frequencies and the tip (apex) low ones, so different notes light up different points along the coil.

Vestibule.name: Vestibule (Balance)
Vestibule.function: The central chamber of the inner ear and the heart of your balance system. Its sensors detect gravity and head movement, telling the brain which way is up and how you are moving. It works with the cochlea but serves balance rather than hearing — which is why an inner-ear problem can make you dizzy.
